name: Senda del Hornillo
name_original: Embalse de la Aceña y Cascada del hornillo — circular por el Río Aceña - Parque Nacional de la Sierra de Guadarrama
description: Jog the Senda del Hornillo, a difficult 5.2-mile (8.4 km) loop in Sierra de Guadarrama, featuring the Hornillo Waterfall and Aceña Reservoir
region_intro: Jogging routes around Santa María De La Alameda are set within the Sierra de Guadarrama mountains, offering diverse natural landscapes for outdoor activity. The region features a varied terrain with granite rock formations, wooded pastures, and fertile river valleys carved by the Alberche, Cofio, and Perales rivers. This area is also part of a Special Protection Area for Birds, highlighting its ecological importance. Joggers can explore a mix of mountainous formations and expansive green areas. Best jogging routes around Santa María De La Alameda The most popular jogging route is Seat of Philip II and La Herrería Forest Loop, a 3.6 miles (5.8 km) trail that takes 44 minutes to complete. This route offers views of the historic Seat of Philip II and winds through the La Herrería Forest. Another top favourite among local runners is Senda del Hornillo, a difficult 5.2 miles (8.4 km) path. This trail follows a river and ascends through forest tracks, leading to the impressive Chorrera del Hornillo waterfall. Local runners also love the Abantos – Mount Abantos loop from San Lorenzo de El Escorial, a 5.7 miles (9.1 km) trail leading through mountainous terrain, often completed in about 1 hour 45 minutes. Jogging around Santa María De La Alameda is defined by mountain peaks, river valleys, and granite formations. The network offers options for different ability levels, from easier paths to more challenging ascents. The routes in Santa María De La Alameda are highly rated by the komoot community with an average score of 4.4 stars from over 24 reviews. More than 1800 runners have used komoot to explore Santa María De La Alameda's varied terrain.
region_faq:
Q: How many running routes are available in Santa María De La Alameda?
A: Santa María De La Alameda offers a wide selection of over &lt;strong&gt;130 running routes&lt;/strong&gt;, catering to various preferences and fitness levels. The terrain is diverse, ranging from river valleys to mountainous paths within the Sierra de Guadarrama.

Q: Are there easy running routes suitable for beginners in Santa María De La Alameda?
A: Yes, for those new to running or looking for a less strenuous experience, there are &lt;strong&gt;8 easy running routes&lt;/strong&gt; available in Santa María De La Alameda. These paths often wind through expansive green areas and offer a gentle introduction to the region's natural beauty.

Q: Can I find routes with waterfalls in the Santa María De La Alameda area?
A: Absolutely! The region is home to the impressive Chorrera del Hornillo waterfall. You can explore it via routes like the &lt;a href="https://www.komoot.com/smarttour/e1165008101/senda-del-hornillo" target="_blank" rel="noopener"&gt;Senda del Hornillo&lt;/a&gt;, which follows a river and ascends through forest tracks, offering a rewarding experience for nature lovers.

Q: Are there any circular running routes in Santa María De La Alameda?
A: Many of the running paths in Santa María De La Alameda are circular, allowing you to start and finish at the same point. A popular option is the &lt;a href="https://www.komoot.com/smarttour/e957916232/seat-of-philip-ii-and-la-herreria-forest-loop" target="_blank" rel="noopener"&gt;Seat of Philip II and La Herrería Forest Loop&lt;/a&gt;, which offers scenic views and a varied terrain.

Q: What kind of natural landscapes can I expect to see while running here?
A: The region boasts diverse natural landscapes, including granite rock formations, dehesas (wooded pastures) with holm oaks, and fertile river valleys carved by the Alberche, Cofio, and Perales rivers. You'll encounter a mix of mountainous formations and expansive green areas, providing a rich ecological environment for your run.

Q: Are there any historical or notable landmarks along the running trails?
A: Yes, the area is rich in history and notable landmarks. You can run past sites like the &lt;a href="https://www.komoot.com/highlight/740607" target="_blank" rel="noopener"&gt;Seat of Philip II&lt;/a&gt;, a granite viewpoint offering spectacular panoramic views. Other attractions include &lt;a href="https://www.komoot.com/highlight/2505177" target="_blank" rel="noopener"&gt;Mount Abantos&lt;/a&gt; and the &lt;a href="https://www.komoot.com/highlight/4318799" target="_blank" rel="noopener"&gt;Royal Monastery of San Lorenzo de El Escorial&lt;/a&gt; in the broader area.

Q: What do other runners enjoy most about the trails in Santa María De La Alameda?
A: The running routes in Santa María De La Alameda are highly rated by the komoot community, with an average score of &lt;strong&gt;4.4 stars from over 24 reviews&lt;/strong&gt;. Runners often praise the stunning scenery, the variety of terrain from mountain peaks to river valleys, and the well-defined paths that cater to different ability levels.

Q: Are there running routes that are suitable for families?
A: While specific 'family-friendly' designations aren't always explicit, many of the easier and moderate routes, particularly those along river valleys or through less challenging wooded areas, can be enjoyed by families. Look for routes with lower elevation gain and shorter distances for a comfortable family outing.

Q: Can I bring my dog on the running trails in Santa María De La Alameda?
A: Many natural trails in Spain, including those in Santa María De La Alameda, are generally dog-friendly, especially if your dog is on a leash. However, it's always advisable to check local regulations or specific route details for any restrictions, particularly in protected areas or during certain seasons.

Q: What is the best time of year for running in Santa María De La Alameda?
A: Santa María De La Alameda experiences a temperate Mediterranean climate. Spring and autumn generally offer the most pleasant temperatures for running, with lush landscapes and comfortable conditions. Summer can be warm, especially at midday, while winter offers cooler, crisp air, though some higher elevation paths might be affected by weather.

Q: Are there opportunities for wildlife spotting while running?
A: Yes, the area is part of a Special Protection Area for Birds (ZEPA), known as 'Encinares del río Alberche y río Cofio.' This highlights its ecological importance and rich biodiversity. Runners might spot various bird species, including the Iberian Imperial Eagle and Black Vulture, especially in quieter, less trafficked areas.

Q: How challenging are the running routes in this region?
A: The routes in Santa María De La Alameda offer a good mix of challenges. While there are 8 easy routes, the majority are classified as &lt;strong&gt;moderate (45 routes) or difficult (79 routes)&lt;/strong&gt;, featuring significant elevation changes and varied terrain. For example, the &lt;a href="https://www.komoot.com/smarttour/33932443" target="_blank" rel="noopener"&gt;Abantos – Mount Abantos loop&lt;/a&gt; is a difficult trail with substantial elevation gain.

faqs.question: Is the Senda del Hornillo suitable for jogging beginners?
faqs.answer: While the Senda del Hornillo is officially rated as difficult for jogging due to its overall elevation changes and varied terrain, some sections, particularly leading up to the waterfall, are considered easier. The full circular route involves continuous ascents and descents with a cumulative elevation gain of nearly 300 meters. Beginners should be prepared for irregular surfaces, including stones and roots, especially closer to the waterfall, and potentially slippery ground after rain.

faqs.question: What is the terrain like on the Senda del Hornillo?
faqs.answer: The terrain on this route is quite varied. You'll find a mix of surfaces, starting often parallel to the Hornillo stream on forest tracks. Sections pass through pine forests with comfortable paths, sometimes paved. However, closer to the Hornillo Waterfall, the path becomes more irregular with stones and roots. Expect continuous ascents and descents, and be aware that the ground can be slippery, especially after rainfall.

faqs.question: What natural features and landmarks can I see along the route?
faqs.answer: The Senda del Hornillo offers a rich natural environment. Key highlights include the soothing sound of the Hornillo stream, which accompanies much of the route, and the impressive 10-meter-high Hornillo Waterfall. You'll also pass by the La Aceña Reservoir and traverse diverse forests of pine, oak, and poplar. Panoramic views of the reservoir, surrounding villages, and the imposing Sierra de Gredos can be enjoyed from higher points.

faqs.question: What is the best time of year to jog the Senda del Hornillo?
faqs.answer: The Senda del Hornillo is enjoyable year-round, but it's particularly recommended in spring to witness the Hornillo Waterfall at its fullest flow. Autumn also offers vibrant forest colors, making it a beautiful time to visit. During winter, some sections might be slippery or icy, requiring extra caution.

faqs.question: Where can I park to access the Senda del Hornillo trailhead?
faqs.answer: The Senda del Hornillo is a circular route, often starting near the Puente de la Aceña on the M-535 road in Santa María de la Alameda. Parking is typically available in this vicinity, providing convenient access to the trailhead.

faqs.question: Can I reach the trailhead by public transport?
faqs.answer: While the trail is located in a natural area, public transport options to Santa María de la Alameda, the nearest town, may be limited. It's advisable to check local bus schedules or consider driving for the most direct access to the trailhead.

faqs.question: Is the Senda del Hornillo trail dog-friendly?
faqs.answer: Yes, the Senda del Hornillo is generally dog-friendly. However, as it passes through natural areas and potentially the Sierra de Guadarrama National Park, it's always best to keep your dog on a leash to protect wildlife and ensure the safety of other trail users. Be mindful of the varied terrain, which can be challenging for some dogs.

faqs.question: Are there any permits or entrance fees required for the Senda del Hornillo or Sierra de Guadarrama National Park?
faqs.answer: Generally, there are no specific permits or entrance fees required to access the Senda del Hornillo or to jog within the Sierra de Guadarrama National Park. However, visitors are expected to respect park regulations, such as staying on marked paths, not disturbing wildlife, and carrying out all trash. Always check the official park website for any updated regulations before your visit.

faqs.question: Is the Senda del Hornillo a loop route?
faqs.answer: Yes, the Senda del Hornillo is a well-marked circular route. This means you will return to your starting point without having to retrace your steps, making it convenient for joggers.

faqs.question: Does the Senda del Hornillo intersect with other trails?
faqs.answer: Yes, the route incorporates sections of other official trails. You will find yourself on parts of the "Senda del Hornillo" (74% of the route), "Camino de Hornillo" (23% and 8% in different sections), and "Camino de Robledondo" (7%) during your jog.

faqs.question: What should I bring for jogging on this trail?
faqs.answer: Given the varied and sometimes challenging terrain, we recommend wearing appropriate trail running shoes with good grip. Bring sufficient water, especially on warmer days, as there are no facilities on the trail. A light backpack with snacks, a first-aid kit, and a fully charged phone are also advisable. Consider trekking poles for added stability on steeper or slippery sections.
